channel.title: DeployHub User Guide on DeployHub Documentation
channel.description: Recent content in DeployHub User Guide on DeployHub Documentation

item.title: Welcome
item.description:
Welcome to the DeployHub Project

Have you ever wanted to contribute to the coolest cloud technology? Well here is your chance. The DeployHub Open Source Project’s mission is fortify the software supply chain through a world class Continuous Security Intelligence solution, driven by a supportive and diverse global open source community. Watch this video on how to get started.

What is DeployHub?

The DeployHub Continuous Security Intelligence solution is an open-source project focused on surveilling the DevOps pipeline collecting clues and forensics about the software you deliver to end users from Software Bills of Materials (SBOM) to deployment metadata.

item.title: Things You Should Know
item.description:
Getting Started

Before submitting code to DeployHub, there are a few things you should know. From completing a contributor license agreement to understanding the different levels of participation, the below information will help you get started.

Sign the CLA

If you would like to contribute code, documentation content, blogs or graphics you will need to complete the Linux Foundation DeployHub Individual Easy Contributor License Agreement

If your organization would like to become a contributing organization, please have the appropriate individual complete the Linux Foundation DeployHub Corporate Easy Contributor License Agreement. We welcome organizations of any size to be part of solving the microservice puzzle and encourage you to get your organization involved.

item.title: Adventure with Abraham Ortelius
item.description:
— Adventure with Abraham Ortelius —

Motivation

  • This is a navigational map in the spirit of Abraham Ortelius
  • Please read and explore the documentation for each subject
  • Google is your friend for when you get lost
  • To answer your question “Yes it is hard” but you can do it
  • If Abraham Ortelius could draw maps in the 1500s without our modern day technology you can do this
  • Abraham Ortelius was a DevOps Engineer and Microservices pioneer by containerising the world through cartography
  • Please help us improve the cartography

Organizations

  • CNCF
  • CNCF Cloud Native Landscape
  • DeployHub

What are we learning?

  • Backend development in Go
  • Frontend development with Node.js and Next.js
  • Containerization with Docker or Podman
  • ArangoDB
  • Kubernetes
  • Build pipelines
  • DeployHub
  • Cloud Computing
  • Argo CD
  • Software Supply Chain security and SBOMs

What are we building?

DeployHub PDVD — Package, Dependency, Vulnerability Dashboard

DeployHub tracks SBOMs through the entire release lifecycle, mapping components to CVEs so teams can rapidly respond to supply chain threats.

item.title: Community Membership
item.description:
There are many ways to contribute to the DeployHub project. Here are the various levels of involvement and how to get there!

User

Try out DeployHub with the SaaS version hosted by DeployHub

  • Open an issue for any gaps in the documentation that you encounter.
  • File a bug when unexpected things happen.
  • Take a look at some of our open proposals and provide feedback.
  • Answer questions of other users in the community.

Member

Project members are people who are interested in contributing further.

item.title: Running the Go Backend Locally
item.description:
Running the ortelius Locally

The DeployHub backend (ortelius/ortelius) is a Go REST API that tracks SBOMs through the release lifecycle and maps them to CVEs.

Tools Needed

  • Go (1.26 or later)
  • VS Code with the Go extension
  • Docker (for the ArangoDB test database)
  • Git
  • Linux, macOS, or Windows with WSL2

Clone the Repository

cd /path/to/working_directory
git clone https://github.com/ortelius/ortelius.git
cd ortelius

Install Dependencies

go mod download

Start the Test Database

Pull and run ArangoDB locally:

item.title: Running the Next.js Frontend Locally
item.description:
Running the frontend Locally

The DeployHub frontend (ortelius/frontend) is a Next.js application that provides the web UI for the Package, Dependency, Vulnerability Dashboard.

Tools Needed

  • Node.js LTS (includes npm)
  • VS Code
  • Git
  • Linux, macOS, or Windows with WSL2

Clone the Repository

cd /path/to/working_directory
git clone https://github.com/ortelius/frontend.git
cd frontend

Install Dependencies

npm install

Configure Environment

Copy the example env file and update values as needed:

cp .env.example .env.local

Set NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL (or the equivalent variable in the repo) to point at your local backend:

item.title: Containerising the Go Backend
item.description:
Containerising the ortelius

Before Starting

  • Docker installed and running
  • WSL2 (Windows) or native Linux/macOS terminal
  • The ortelius repo cloned locally

Dockerfile

The repo includes a Dockerfile. A typical multi-stage Go build looks like this:

# Build stage
FROM golang:1.21-alpine AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY go.mod go.sum ./
RUN go mod download
COPY . .
RUN go build -o server .

# Run stage
FROM alpine:latest
WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=builder /app/server .
EXPOSE 8080
ENTRYPOINT ["./server"]

Check the repo’s actual Dockerfile — use that file rather than overwriting it.

item.title: Containerising the Next.js Frontend
item.description:
Containerising the frontend

Before Starting

  • Docker installed and running
  • WSL2 (Windows) or native Linux/macOS terminal
  • The frontend repo cloned locally

Dockerfile

The repo includes a Dockerfile. A typical Next.js multi-stage build:

# Install dependencies
FROM node:20-alpine AS deps
WORKDIR /app
COPY package.json package-lock.json ./
RUN npm ci

# Build
FROM node:20-alpine AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=deps /app/node_modules ./node_modules
COPY . .
RUN npm run build

# Run
FROM node:20-alpine AS runner
WORKDIR /app
ENV NODE_ENV=production
COPY --from=builder /app/.next ./.next
COPY --from=builder /app/node_modules ./node_modules
COPY --from=builder /app/package.json ./package.json
EXPOSE 3000
CMD ["npm", "start"]

Check the repo’s actual Dockerfile — use that file rather than overwriting it.

item.title: Fine dining with Abraham Ortelius
item.description:
Fine dining with Abraham Ortelius

Canapes

Windows 10 / 11

  • Latest version & fully updated
  • Enable WSL2 | Go to Programs and features → Turn Windows features on or off → Windows Subsystem for Linux
  • Install Windows Terminal for easier management
  • Use this documentation to install the latest Ubuntu flavour (we recommend Ubuntu Jammy JellyFish)
  • Line feed ending issues can be solved with the Change All End of Line Sequence VS Code extension or Dos2Unix
  • Windows uses CRLF; Linux uses LF
  • Suggestion: Set your VS Code to LF and do all your work inside WSL2

WSL Configs

Windows: c:\Users\Abraham\.wslconfig

item.title: Kind
item.description:
Kind.sigs.k8s.io

  • Kind allows you to use Docker to run K8s nodes as containers
  • Install here
  • Get familiar with the basic commands
  • Checkout the Kind documentation here

Why kind?

  • kind supports multi-node (including HA) clusters
  • kind supports building Kubernetes release builds from source
  • support for make / bash or docker, in addition to pre-published builds
  • kind supports Linux, macOS and Windows
  • kind is a CNCF certified conformant Kubernetes installer

Kubernetes.io

  • K8s is a production grade container orchestrater
  • K8s is the conductor of the orchestra where the conductor is K8s and the orchestra is the containers within PODs
  • PODS are cuddly safe worlds to hold containers (so cute)
  • Get familiar with the basic commands

Requirements

  • Docker installed & running
  • Kubectl command line tool

Up & Running

Clusters

  • Create a cluster kind create cluster
  • Delete a cluster kind delete cluster

Kubectl

  • Install kubectl the command line tool here
  • Use the kubectl cheat sheet here
  • Use Devdocs and the official documentation here
  • Add the aliases & auto complete which are in the cheat sheet
  • Interacting with your cluster
  • Switch context and namespaces easily with these tools

kind-ortelius-config.yaml

item.title: Localstack
item.description:
AWS Localstack The AWS Cloud on your local machine OPTIONAL

  • A fully functional local cloud stack to Develop and test your cloud and serverless apps offline!
  • AWS only at the time of writing
  • Getting started
  • Localstack Docs
  • Integrations
  • Localstack Cockpit Web UI use this to make sure your services are running
  • AWS Feature Coverage

Integrations

  • Serverless Framework
  • Spring Cloud Function
  • Architect
  • AWS Copilot CLI
  • Terraform
  • Thundra
  • AWS SAM
  • AWS CDK
  • Pulumi
  • Kafka cluster
  • AWS Chalice

item.title: Connect GitHub
item.description:
DeployHub connects to GitHub via a GitHub App installation, which grants access to specific repositories without requiring a personal access token per user.

User-facing flow

  1. From the Welcome page or Profile → Connect GitHub, the user is redirected to GitHub to install the DeployHub GitHub App.
  2. The user selects which repositories to grant access to.
  3. GitHub redirects back to DeployHub with the installation connected, and the installation ID is stored against the user (and optionally the org).

Self-hosted configuration

To offer this flow on a self-hosted deployment, create your own GitHub App:

item.title: Developer Resources
item.description:
Developer Tools

The following tools are needed to contribute to DeployHub code or documentation.

Editors

Visual Studio Code is the recommended editor. Install the following extensions:

  • Go — Go Team at Google (golang.go)
  • ESLint — Microsoft (dbaeumer.vscode-eslint)
  • Prettier — Prettier (esbenp.prettier-vscode)
  • Markdown Preview Enhanced — Yiyi Wang
  • markdownlint — David Anderson
  • Docker — Microsoft
  • YAML — Red Hat

Backend Development (Go)

The backend lives at ortelius/ortelius and is written in Go.

item.title: Executive Summary Cards
item.description:
Five cards give you the headline numbers at a glance:

Card What it means Calculation
Total New CVEs Total vulnerabilities detected within the rolling 180-day window CVEs where Detected Date is within the last 180 days
Post-Deploy CVEs Currently open CVEs that were disclosed after the software was already deployed Open CVEs where Disclosure Date > Deployment date
MTTR (Pre + Post Deploy) Average days to remediate across all endpoint CVEs fixed in the period Σ(Fix Date − First Introduced Date) / Total Fixed CVEs
MTTR (Post-Deploy) Average days to remediate for post-deployment CVEs only Σ(Fix Date − First Introduced Date) / Total Fixed Post-Deploy CVEs. Clock starts at root_introduced_at — the first known version where the CVE was present
% Open > SLA Percentage of open CVEs exceeding their severity-based SLA (Count of Open CVEs > SLA / Total Open CVEs) × 100

See SLA Reference for how SLA targets are defined per severity.

item.title: Hosted SaaS
item.description:
The hosted version at app.deployhub.com requires no infrastructure setup. Sign up, connect GitHub, and you have real vulnerability data within minutes.

This is the right choice if:

  • You want to evaluate DeployHub quickly
  • You don’t need to self-manage the database, backend, or scanner components
  • Your data can live in the hosted environment

Sign up

  1. Go to app.deployhub.com and click Sign Up
  2. Enter your username, email, first name, last name, and organization name
  3. Check your email for an invitation link — click it to set your password and log in
  4. You’re in. Your organization is created and you are its owner.

Note: If your organization already exists in the system, you’ll see a conflict message with the org admin’s email address. Contact them directly to be added.

item.title: How DeployHub Objects Relate
item.description:
The Problem: Graph Explosion

The naive approach to connecting vulnerability data with software inventory is to draw a direct edge from every CVE to every SBOM that contains the affected package. This creates an N×M edge problem:

graph LR
    subgraph CVEs["1,000 CVEs"]
        C1[CVE-1] & C2[CVE-2] & C3[CVE-3]
    end
    subgraph SBOMs["10,000 SBOMs"]
        S1[SBOM-1] & S2[SBOM-2] & S3[SBOM-3] & S4[...]
    end

    C1 -.-> S1 & S2 & S3 & S4
    C2 -.-> S1 & S2 & S3 & S4
    C3 -.-> S1 & S2 & S3 & S4

    COST["⚠️ 10,000,000 edges\n~500 MB metadata\nO(M) scan per query"]

    style COST fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828

At scale this creates three compounding problems: storage (millions of edges with duplicate metadata), write amplification (adding one new CVE requires creating thousands of edges), and query performance (finding all SBOMs for a CVE requires scanning the entire edge set).

item.title: How To Tips
item.description:
Opening an Issue

GitHub Issues are used to track all bugs, enhancement requests, and “todos.” DeployHub is spread across many repositories so the ortelius/ortelius repo is used for all issues. Issues should have a link, using markdown []() link format, to the true repository in which the bug, enhancement, or “todo” needs to be made.

Please be detailed in your description of the issue. Not everything needs to go in the summary line. Feel free to use the description area to provide additional details.

item.title: Manage Organizations
item.description:
Each org stores its own display name, description, and repo-tracking configuration:

{
  "name": "acme",
  "display_name": "ACME Corporation",
  "description": "Main enterprise customer",
  "is_public": false,
  "metadata": { "tier": "enterprise" },
  "tracked_repos": [ /* ... */ ],
  "hidden_repos": []
}

Credentials

An org owner can store a GitHub or GitLab personal access token directly on the org via POST /api/v1/orgs/:org/credentials, as an alternative or supplement to the GitHub App installation. Tokens are encrypted at rest and never returned by the API once stored — only connection-status booleans are exposed via GET /api/v1/orgs/:org/status.

item.title: OCI Attestations
item.description:
An OCI Attestation is an SBOM or other artifact attached directly to a container image in the registry. This is the first source DeployHub’ scanner components check for a container-image release:

  1. OCI Referrers API — looks for an attached artifact with a media type containing sbom or cyclonedx.
  2. Cosign DSSE attestation — looks for a DSSE envelope referrer and decodes the predicate.

If neither is present, the scanner falls back to a GitHub Release Asset, or generates one with Syft or cdxgen.

item.title: Pull Request (PR) Cheat Sheet
item.description:
Savoir-vivre of pull requests

Patches for fixes, features, and improvements are accepted through pull requests. Here are some tips for contributing:

  • Add Git Commit Signing to your local git install and to GitHub. Here are the config instructions.
  • Write good commit messages in the present tense (“Add X”, not “Added X”) with a short title, blank line, and bullet points if needed. Capitalize the first letter of the title and any bullet items. No punctuation in the title.
  • Include tests to improve coverage and prevent regressions.
  • Squash changes into a single commit per feature/fix. Typical steps to do that are:
    • git rebase -i HEAD~3 (the number depends on the number of commits you are squashing)
    • git push -u origin master –force (master might not be the branch you are pushing to so make sure to change to the branch)
  • Whenever possible, tag your pull request with appropriate Github labels and issue numbers.

Important: For any breaking changes that require a major version bump, add BREAKING CHANGE somewhere in the commit title or message.

item.title: Search for Packages
item.description:
Use the Name/Package filter (text-based search) to find a specific package across all your tracked releases, or search by exact CVE ID if you already know which vulnerability you’re chasing.

Package identity in DeployHub is based on a standardized PURL (Package URL) — see the Glossary — so the same package is matched consistently whether it comes from a CVE record (OSV data) or an SBOM component (CycloneDX data).

From a package search result, you can jump to:

item.title: What DeployHub Does
item.description:
When a critical open source vulnerability is disclosed, most security teams face the same four questions in rapid succession — and struggle to answer any of them quickly:

  • What exactly is the threat? The CVE ID, CVSS score, and enough detail to know whether it’s exploitable in your environment.
  • Where in your codebase do you fix it? Which repo, which package, and which version introduced the vulnerable dependency.
  • Where is that code actually running right now? Not where it was deployed last quarter — where it is live in production today, whether that’s a Kubernetes cluster, a cloud function, or an edge device in the field.
  • How do you actually fix it? The upgraded version and a clear remediation path so nothing falls through the cracks.

DeployHub answers all four questions in a single platform. It ingests your Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) at build time, matches your deployed components against the OSV vulnerability database every 15 minutes, tracks every CVE from the moment it is introduced into a release through every environment it reaches until it is remediated, and measures how long each step takes against your SLA targets.

item.title: VS Code Setup for Backend Development
item.description:
Getting Started

The DeployHub backend (ortelius/ortelius) is written in Go. Visual Studio Code is the recommended editor for all backend work.

Prerequisites

  • Go (1.26 or later)
  • VS Code
  • Git
  • Docker (to run ArangoDB locally)

Recommended VS Code Extensions

Install these from the Extensions panel (Ctrl+Shift+X):

Extension Publisher Purpose
Go Go Team at Google (golang.go) Language support, IntelliSense, debugging
REST Client Huachao Mao (humao.rest-client) Test API endpoints from inside VS Code
Docker Microsoft Container management
YAML Red Hat YAML file support
markdownlint David Anderson Docs linting
Change All End of Line Sequence vs-publisher-1448185 Fix CRLF/LF issues
Todo Tree Gruntfuggly Track TODOs

Setting Up the Workspace

  1. Clone the repo:

item.title: GitHub Release Assets
item.description:
For repositories that publish GitHub Releases directly (rather than, or in addition to, container images), relscanner-job looks for a release asset whose name matches an SBOM/CycloneDX pattern and downloads it.

If the matched asset isn’t itself a valid SBOM (for example, a Java build artifact), the scanner generates one from it using Syft or cdxgen instead. There is special handling for Java build artifacts specifically, since these are a common case where the “SBOM-shaped” release asset needs further processing.

item.title: GitLab Integration
item.description:
Unlike GitHub, DeployHub does not currently offer a GitLab App installation flow — GitLab repositories are connected using a personal access token (PAT) instead.

Org-level token

An org owner stores a GitLab PAT directly on the org via POST /api/v1/orgs/:org/credentials, encrypted at rest with TOKEN_ENCRYPTION_KEY. Once configured, GitLab repos can be added the same way as GitHub repos — see Manage Organizations.

System-wide public tracked repos

Public GitLab repositories can be favorited by name by any authenticated user via POST /api/v1/tracked-repos, using a system-wide GITLAB_TOKEN fallback — no org credential required.

item.title: Manage Users and Roles
item.description:
For inviting your first colleagues, see Invite Your Team. This page covers ongoing administration.

Roles

Role Can do
Owner Everything, including managing billing and deleting the org
Admin Everything except billing — invite users, manage roles, access all resources
Editor Upload releases, upload SBOMs, sync endpoints — the right role for CI/CD service accounts
Viewer Read-only — dashboards, CVE details, endpoint status

A user’s stored role is the highest role they hold across any org membership. See Orgs for the full role hierarchy model.

item.title: Onboard Repositories
item.description:
From Profile → GitHub Integration, a user picks which of their installed repositories to onboard (POST /api/v1/github/onboard). For each selected owner/repo, DeployHub:

  1. Fetches GitHub Releases for the repo via the App installation token and creates a release record per release tag (version = tag name; visibility is set from the repo’s public/private status).
  2. Fetches recent GitHub Actions workflow runs and, for each run that completed successfully, creates a github-actions/<owner>/<repo> endpoint and a sync record (release version = first 7 characters of the run’s head commit SHA).

This gives you a release and deployment timeline for the repo. Repeated onboarding calls simply re-run this same import — this REST endpoint itself has no automatic SBOM discovery, container image inspection, or OpenSSF Scorecard lookup.

item.title: Orgs
item.description:
An Org is the top-level tenant boundary in DeployHub. Every release, endpoint, and sync record carries an org field, and access is scoped accordingly.

Org Isolation

Queries filter by the requesting user’s orgs[] array. Users with an empty orgs: [] array have global access — the pattern used for system administrators.

Org names are normalized to lowercase throughout the system. display_name preserves original casing for display purposes.

Role Hierarchy

User role is stored on the user document and is the highest role the user holds across all org memberships.

item.title: Review CVE Details
item.description:
The Vulnerabilities view is DeployHub’ CVE database with CVSS scoring, filterable by severity (Critical, High, Medium, Low, Clean) and CVE ID.

Opening a specific CVE shows:

  • Its CVSS base score and severity rating (see CVSS Score Calculation in Core Concepts for how this is derived)
  • Which of your releases include the affected package version
  • Whether it was disclosed before or after those releases were deployed (disclosed_after_deployment)

For remediation-focused triage rather than CVE-by-CVE browsing, use the Mitigations view instead, which surfaces open vulnerabilities in an actionable, work-queue format.

item.title: Self-Hosted
item.description:
Self-hosting DeployHub means running the backend, database (ArangoDB), and optional scanner components (relscanner-job, osvdev-job, deployment-gke) yourself, typically on Kubernetes.

This is the right choice if:

  • You need full control over data residency
  • You’re operating in a regulated or air-gapped environment
  • You want to customize or extend the platform’s deployment topology

Platform engineers, infrastructure teams, and security reviewers evaluating a self-hosted deployment should also read the Architecture Guide for the full non-functional requirements, technology stack, and deployment architecture.

item.title: Severity Breakdown and SLA Compliance
item.description:
A table segmented by Critical, High, Medium, and Low with six columns per row:

Column What it means
MTTR (Days) Σ(Fix − Detect) / Fixed — average remediation time for closed CVEs at this severity
MTTR (Post) Σ(Fix − Detect) / Post-deploy fixed — same but restricted to post-deployment CVEs
% Fixed in SLA (Fixed ≤ SLA / Total) × 100 — what fraction were resolved before the deadline
Mean Age Σ(Now − Detect) / Open — average age of currently open CVEs at this severity
Oldest Max open age — the single oldest unresolved CVE at this severity
% > SLA (Open > SLA / Total Open) × 100 — how many open CVEs have already breached the deadline

See Review SLA Status for how to act on this table, and SLA Reference for the underlying targets.

item.title: VS Code Setup for Frontend Development
item.description:
Getting Started

The DeployHub frontend (ortelius/frontend) is a Next.js application. Visual Studio Code is the recommended editor for all frontend work.

Prerequisites

  • Node.js LTS
  • VS Code
  • Git

Recommended VS Code Extensions

Install these from the Extensions panel (Ctrl+Shift+X):

Extension Publisher Purpose
ESLint Microsoft JavaScript/TypeScript linting
Prettier — Code formatter Prettier Auto-formatting
Markdown Preview Enhanced Yiyi Wang Docs preview
markdownlint David Anderson Docs linting
Docker Microsoft Container management
YAML Red Hat YAML file support
Change All End of Line Sequence vs-publisher-1448185 Fix CRLF/LF issues
Todo Tree Gruntfuggly Track TODOs

Setting Up the Workspace

  1. Clone the repo:

item.title: Who Should Use DeployHub
item.description:
DeployHub is aimed at teams who need to know not just that a vulnerability exists, but where it’s actually running and how urgently it needs to be fixed.

Security teams

DeployHub gives security teams a single, continuously updated view of open vulnerabilities across every deployed release, with SLA tracking (Critical/High/Medium/Low targets) and MTTR metrics so you can report on remediation progress rather than just detection.

Platform and infrastructure teams

If you run Kubernetes clusters, GKE, or other production environments, DeployHub’ post-deployment tracking tells you what’s actually live right now — not what was deployed last quarter — by syncing endpoint and release data automatically.

item.title: Configure SLA Policy
item.description:
By default, every dashboard and SLA calculation reflects the NIST Recommended SLA Policy:

Severity Standard Endpoint Mission-Critical Endpoint
Critical 15 days 7 days
High 30 days 15 days
Medium 90 days 90 days
Low 180 days 180 days

Org admins can configure a different policy for their organization if the NIST defaults don’t match internal compliance requirements. Once changed, every SLA-based metric on the dashboard — Executive Summary Cards, Severity Breakdown and SLA Compliance, and Security Velocity and Impact Metrics — recalculates against the new targets.

item.title: Deployment Tracking Using GitHub and GitLab
item.description:
The Release Scanner (relscanner-job) is a Kubernetes CronJob that automatically discovers new software releases from GitHub and GitLab, enriches each with SBOM data, Git provenance, and OpenSSF Scorecard results, and syncs the release records to the DeployHub/DeployHub backend (ortelius) via the /api/v1/releases API.

This component runs alongside the DeployHub backend and complements the Deployment Tracking for GKE capability by capturing releases at their source — the CI/CD pipeline / release process — rather than at runtime cluster observation.

item.title: GitHub Actions Deployment Tracking
item.description:
When a repository is onboarded (see Onboard Repositories), DeployHub inspects recent GitHub Actions workflow runs. For each run that completed successfully, it creates:

  • A github-actions/<owner>/<repo> endpoint
  • A sync record, with the release version set to the first 7 characters of the run’s head commit SHA

This is a lightweight way to get some deployment signal without deploying a separate scanner component, but it only reflects that a workflow ran successfully — not that a specific artifact reached a specific runtime environment. For deeper deployment tracking that follows a release into GitHub/GitLab CI/CD and into running container images, see Deployment Tracking Using GitHub and GitLab and, for GKE specifically, Deployment Tracking for GKE.

item.title: Repositories
item.description:
A repository itself isn’t a first-class stored object in DeployHub — instead, an org’s tracked_repos list records which GitHub or GitLab repositories are being watched, and each repository produces the objects that are stored: Releases, and — where CI runs succeed — Endpoints and Sync Records.

{
  "tracked_repos": [
    { "provider": "github", "owner": "acme", "name": "payment-service", "private": true, "added_by": "alice", "added_at": "2024-11-01T10:00:00Z" }
  ]
}

A repo gets tracked one of three ways, corresponding to the three onboarding passes described in Select Repositories to Watch and Deployment Tracking Using GitHub and GitLab:

item.title: Review Blast Radius / Impact
item.description:
Blast radius is how far a vulnerable component’s impact actually reaches — every deployed endpoint running a release that includes it, not just the release itself. See the Glossary for the short definition.

From a CVE or package, DeployHub traces:

Release → Endpoint, using the sync records that describe what’s currently deployed where, so you can see every Kubernetes cluster, VM, or serverless endpoint affected by a given vulnerability — not just the code repository it originated in.

item.title: Review SLA Status
item.description:
SLA targets define how many days your team has to remediate a CVE before it counts as overdue. The clock starts when the CVE is first detected on a deployed endpoint.

Severity Standard Endpoint Mission-Critical Endpoint
Critical 15 days 7 days
High 30 days 15 days
Medium 90 days 90 days
Low 180 days 180 days

Endpoints marked as mission-critical use the tighter targets in the right column — see Configure Mission-Critical Endpoints.

item.title: Syft
item.description:
Syft is used as an on-the-fly SBOM generator whenever no OCI attestation or usable GitHub Release asset is found.

  • Container image path: the scanner pulls the container image and generates a CycloneDX JSON SBOM locally.
  • GitHub Release path: Syft (or cdxgen) runs against the downloaded release asset, or as a final fallback against a full clone of the repo’s source tree.

Syft is also used by the GKE deployment tracker as its fallback when no Cosign attestation is attached to a deployed image.

item.title: Volume and Flow
item.description: Shows the Backlog Delta — new CVEs detected minus CVEs fixed — broken out as a bar chart by severity (Critical, High, Medium, Low). Green bars are fixes; red bars are new CVEs. A rising red bar means risk is accumulating faster than it is being resolved. The aggregate delta number is shown top-right; positive means the backlog is growing.

item.title: cdxgen
item.description:
cdxgen is used as a further fallback SBOM generator, particularly for C/C++ repositories that Syft can’t characterize well.

relscanner-job detects build markers (CMakeLists.txt, Makefile, etc.) in a clone of the repository and runs cdxgen against it when:

  • No OCI attestation or GitHub Release asset SBOM is found, and
  • Syft is not well-suited to the project’s build system

This is the last tier in the SBOM acquisition priority order for both the container-image and GitHub-Release acquisition paths.

item.title: Configure Endpoints
item.description:
Most endpoints are created automatically as a byproduct of deployment tracking (see Track Deployments), but you can also configure their metadata directly:

{
  "name": "prod-us-east-1",
  "org": "acme",
  "endpoint_type": "eks",
  "environment": "production",
  "is_public": true
}
  • endpoint_type — Kubernetes, Docker, VM, or Serverless, used for filtering on the dashboard.
  • environment — Production, Staging, Development, or Test.

See Endpoints for the full object model, and Configure Mission-Critical Endpoints for tightening SLA targets on specific endpoints.

item.title: Deployment Tracking for GKE
item.description:
A Google Cloud Function that automatically detects new pod deployments in GKE clusters by tailing Cloud Audit Logs, then syncs release metadata — including SBOMs, Git provenance, and container image details — to a DeployHub / DeployHub backend via the /api/v1/sync API.

What It Does

  • Polls GCP Cloud Audit Logs for pods.create events across all GKE clusters in a project
  • Extracts image references, tags, namespaces, and cluster names from each pod creation event
  • Resolves image digest (SHA) from the container registry
  • Reads OCI image labels (org.opencontainers.image.*) to extract Git source URL and commit SHA
  • Clones the Git repository to enrich the release with branch and commit timestamp
  • Attempts to fetch a CycloneDX SBOM from a Cosign attestation attached to the image; falls back to generating one on-the-fly using Syft
  • Maps cluster/namespace pairs to organization names via environment variables
  • POSTs all release data in a single batch to the DeployHub sync API

Architecture

Cloud Scheduler → Cloud Function (HTTP trigger)
                      ↓
              GCP Cloud Audit Logs
                      ↓
         Image Registry (digest + labels)
                      ↓
          Git Repo (branch + commit info)
                      ↓
        Cosign Attestation / Syft SBOM
                      ↓
         DeployHub /api/v1/sync API

Prerequisites

  • A GCP project with GKE clusters
  • Terraform >= 1.3
  • Go 1.26 (handled automatically by Cloud Build)
  • A running DeployHub or DeployHub instance reachable from the function
  • gcloud CLI authenticated with sufficient permissions

Installation

1. Clone the repository

git clone <your-repo-url>
cd <repo-directory>

2. Configure variables

Copy and edit the tfvars file:

item.title: Log In for the First Time
item.description:
Haven’t created an account or instance yet? See Choose Your Installation Type first.

Signing in

  • Password login — standard email/password with a session-based JWT cookie.
  • Optional SSO — “Sign in with Google” and “Sign in with GitHub” buttons appear automatically if your organization’s admin has configured them on the backend. This is separate from connecting the GitHub App for repository access (see Connect GitHub).

Note: The “Forgot Password” flow currently calls a backend stub that returns success without actually sending a reset email — password resets aren’t functional yet end-to-end. Contact your admin or Get Support if you’re locked out.

item.title: Releases
item.description:
A release is a specific version of a component — a container image, application version, or published release. It’s the object everything else attaches to: SBOMs, CVE matches, and deployment history.

{
  "_key": "acme-payment-service_2.1.0",
  "name": "acme/payment-service",
  "version": "2.1.0",
  "version_major": 2,
  "version_minor": 1,
  "version_patch": 0,
  "version_prerelease": "",
  "org": "acme",
  "shortname": "payment-service",
  "is_public": false,
  "contentsha": "abc123def456",
  "projecttype": "docker",
  "gitcommit": "abc123def456",
  "dockersha": "sha256:...",
  "openssf_scorecard_score": 8.5,
  "objtype": "ProjectRelease"
}

Deduplication

Releases are deduplicated by the composite key (name, version, contentsha), where contentsha comes from gitcommit (preferred) or dockersha. Uploading the same release twice with the same git commit is a no-op; a different git commit creates a new record even with an identical name and version, to handle build-reproducibility cases.

item.title: Review Deployment and DORA Metrics
item.description:
DeployHub tracks deployments as they happen — via GitHub Actions workflow runs, the relscanner-job scanner, or GKE deployment tracking — and surfaces them under the Synced Endpoints view, showing exactly where each release is currently running (Kubernetes, Docker, VMs, or serverless).

This deployment history is also what powers blast radius analysis — knowing where a release is deployed is what lets DeployHub tell you which endpoints a given CVE actually affects.

If your setup doesn’t yet have deployment data flowing, revisit Track Deployments in Start Here.

item.title: Vulnerability Trend
item.description: A stacked area chart showing open CVE counts per day for each severity band over the rolling 180-day window. Use this to spot when a new batch of vulnerabilities was introduced (a sudden step up) or when a remediation effort took effect (a step down). The x-axis is date; the y-axis is count of open CVEs. Each band is colour-coded: Critical (red), High (orange), Medium (yellow), Low (blue).

item.title: Configure Mission-Critical Endpoints
item.description:
Endpoints with endpoint_type: mission_asset use tighter SLA targets than standard endpoints:

Severity Standard Endpoint Mission-Critical Endpoint
Critical 15 days 7 days
High 30 days 15 days
Medium 90 days 90 days
Low 180 days 180 days

Mark your highest-value production endpoints — customer-facing services, payment processing, anything with elevated compliance requirements — as mission-critical so their SLA breaches are surfaced sooner. See Configure Endpoints for the general endpoint configuration, and SLA Reference for how these targets are used across the dashboard.

item.title: Invite Your Team
item.description:
As an org owner or admin, go to your organization settings to invite colleagues.

Each invited user receives an email with a link to set their password. Links expire after 48 hours. If a link expires, an admin can resend it.

When choosing a role, use the minimum necessary:

Role Can do
Owner Everything, including managing billing and deleting the org
Admin Everything except billing — invite users, manage roles, access all resources
Editor Upload releases, upload SBOMs, sync endpoints — the right role for CI/CD service accounts
Viewer Read-only — dashboards, CVE details, endpoint status

For ongoing user and role management beyond the initial invite, see Manage Users and Roles.

item.title: Quick Tour with Sample Data
item.description:
You don’t need to connect your own repositories to see DeployHub working. Both the hosted and self-hosted experiences give you real data to explore immediately after account creation.

Sample data out of the box

After you activate your account, you land on the Welcome page. It automatically adds a few popular public repositories to your Favorites, so there’s real vulnerability, SBOM, and deployment data to explore — no action needed on your part for this step.

item.title: SBOMs
item.description:
An SBOM (Software Bill of Materials) is a machine-readable inventory of every library a release depends on, stored as CycloneDX JSON.

{
  "_key": "...",
  "contentsha": "sha256:...",
  "objtype": "SBOM",
  "content": { "...": "CycloneDX JSON" }
}

SBOMs are deduplicated by SHA256 content hash and linked to a release via the release2sbom edge. Each package inside an SBOM is linked to a version-free PURL hub node via sbom2purl, which is how CVE matching finds candidates.

How SBOM data reaches a release

See Attach or Discover SBOMs for the setup-flow explanation, and SBOM Integration for the full list of sources DeployHub can pull an SBOM from (OCI attestations, GitHub Release assets, Syft, cdxgen).

item.title: Security Velocity and Impact Metrics
item.description:
Four metric cards focused on operational effectiveness:

Metric What it means Calculation
Fix Velocity CVEs remediated per week over the last 180 days Fixed CVEs / 26 weeks
High-Risk Backlog Total open Critical + High CVEs right now Count of open Critical + open High
Shift-Left Success Percentage of CVEs caught before they reached production Pre-Deploy / (Pre-Deploy + Total) × 100
SLA Burn Rate How many SLA deadlines are being breached per 30-day period Open CVEs where (SLA − Age) < 30d

Three summary numbers appear at the bottom of this section:

item.title: CVEs
item.description:
A CVE document represents a single vulnerability, sourced from OSV.dev and refreshed every 15 minutes.

{
  "_key": "CVE-2024-1234",
  "id": "CVE-2024-1234",
  "summary": "Buffer overflow in lodash",
  "published": "2024-11-15T00:00:00Z",
  "database_specific": {
    "cvss_base_score": 9.8,
    "severity_rating": "CRITICAL"
  },
  "affected": [
    {
      "package": { "ecosystem": "npm", "name": "lodash" },
      "ranges": [
        { "type": "SEMVER", "events": [{"introduced": "0"}, {"fixed": "4.17.21"}] }
      ]
    }
  ]
}

CVSS scoring and severity

CVSS vector strings are parsed and pre-calculated at ingestion time using github.com/pandatix/go-cvss, so severity queries use a simple indexed string comparison rather than parsing vectors at query time.

Severity CVSS Score Range Notes
CRITICAL 9.0 – 10.0
HIGH 7.0 – 8.9
MEDIUM 4.0 – 6.9
LOW 0.1 – 3.9 Also assigned to CVEs with missing/unparseable data
NONE 0.0

CVEs without a parseable CVSS vector are assigned severity_rating: "LOW" and cvss_base_score: 0.1 rather than being discarded — this keeps tracking comprehensive rather than silently dropping ambiguous records.

item.title: OpenSSF Scorecard
item.description:
OpenSSF Scorecard is an automated security health score (0–10) for open source repositories, covering things like branch protection, code review practices, and dependency update automation.

relscanner-job fetches a Scorecard result automatically for repositories it scans, via the securityscorecards.dev API. Results are parameterized by platform, so coverage depends on that project’s own GitLab support when scanning GitLab repos.

The backend’s own onboarding endpoints don’t fetch this automatically — if you’re not running relscanner-job, you can populate scorecard_result on a release yourself via the API. See the Releases API.

item.title: Understand How DeployHub Gets Data
item.description:
Before connecting anything, it helps to know what “having data” in DeployHub actually means. There are three layers, and each one arrives a different way:

1. Release and deployment metadata

Comes from connecting GitHub (or GitLab) — see Connect GitHub and Select Repositories to Watch. This gives you release records and, for successful CI runs, deployment/endpoint records. On its own, this does not include vulnerability data.

2. SBOM data

Needed for CVE matching. Reaches DeployHub automatically through a scanner component (typically relscanner-job), with no changes required to your CI/CD pipeline. See Attach or Discover SBOMs.

item.title: Connect GitHub
item.description:
Connecting your GitHub account is the fastest way to get repository and release metadata into DeployHub, though — see the note at the end of this page — it does not by itself give you vulnerability data.

Step 1 — Connect GitHub

  1. On the Welcome page, click Connect GitHub Account (or, from the top-right menu, go to Profile → Connect GitHub).
  2. You’ll be redirected to GitHub to install the DeployHub GitHub App on your account or organization.
  3. Select which repositories to give DeployHub access to — you can start with one and add more later.
  4. After approving, you’ll be redirected back to DeployHub with GitHub connected.

The Profile page also has a GitHub Integration panel for checking connection status and viewing which repos the App can currently see, but repo import/selection itself happens on the Welcome page, not Profile.

item.title: Endpoints
item.description:
An endpoint is a running environment where software is deployed — a Kubernetes cluster, cloud function, VM, or edge device.

{
  "_key": "prod-us-east-1",
  "name": "prod-us-east-1",
  "org": "acme",
  "endpoint_type": "eks",
  "environment": "production",
  "is_public": true,
  "objtype": "Endpoint"
}

Endpoints can be filtered by Status (Active, Inactive, Error), Environment (Production, Staging, Development, Test), and Endpoint Type (Kubernetes, Docker, VM, Serverless) — see Read Your Dashboard.

Endpoints marked endpoint_type: mission_asset use tighter SLA targets — see Configure Mission-Critical Endpoints and SLA Reference.

Endpoints are created automatically as a byproduct of deployment tracking — you generally don’t create them by hand.

item.title: OSV.dev
item.description:
The OSV Loader (osvdev-job) is a Kubernetes CronJob that continuously synchronizes vulnerability intelligence from osv.dev into the DeployHub evidence store. It is the foundation of DeployHub’ post-deployment security model — without it, no CVE matching occurs against your deployed components.

What It Does

On each run the loader fetches the full list of ecosystems published by osv.dev, then for each ecosystem downloads and processes the vulnerability feed. For every new or updated entry it:

item.title: Deployments
item.description:
A deployment, in DeployHub’ data model, is expressed as a sync record connecting a specific release to a specific endpoint at a point in time. There’s no separate “deployment” collection — the sync record itself is the deployment event.

Where deployment data comes from

  • GitHub Actions workflow runs, captured when you connect GitHub
  • The relscanner-job scanner, for GitHub/GitLab CI pipelines
  • The GKE deployment tracker, for Google Kubernetes Engine, via Cloud Audit Logs

See Track Deployments for the setup-flow explanation, and Review Deployment and DORA Metrics for how this data shows up on your dashboard.

item.title: Select Repositories to Watch
item.description:
Once GitHub is connected, choose which repositories DeployHub should actually onboard.

From the Welcome page

  1. Check the boxes next to the repos you want and click Import Selected.
  2. Or, favorite a public repo by name (optional): search by name or owner/repo (GitHub or GitLab) and click Add to Favorites — useful for public repos you don’t have GitHub access to yourself.
  3. Click Go to Organizations at the bottom of the Welcome page — your tracked repos and their security posture appear in the Organizations and Dashboard views.

From Profile → GitHub Integration

You can also pick which of your installed repositories to onboard from Profile → GitHub Integration. For each selected owner/repo, DeployHub:

item.title: Sync Records
item.description:
A sync record is the act of telling DeployHub what version is currently deployed to an endpoint.

{
  "_key": "sync_prod-us-east-1_1733011200",
  "release_name": "acme/payment-service",
  "release_version": "2.1.0",
  "release_version_major": 2,
  "release_version_minor": 1,
  "release_version_patch": 0,
  "endpoint_name": "prod-us-east-1",
  "synced_at": "2024-12-01T00:00:00Z",
  "objtype": "Sync"
}

Partial Sync Strategy

POST /api/v1/sync uses a partial sweep model:

  1. For each release in the payload, all existing lifecycle records for that (endpoint, release) pair are marked is_remediated: true with status Superseded.
  2. CVEs for the new version are fetched from the materialized release2cve edges (see How DeployHub Objects Relate).
  3. Lifecycle records are created or resurrected for each CVE in the new version.
  4. Releases not in the payload are left untouched — not removed.

CVE Lifecycle

Each match between a CVE and a deployed release/endpoint is tracked as a cve_lifecycle record:

item.title: Verify Your Data
item.description:
After onboarding, go to your dashboard. You should see:

  • Your repositories listed under Releases, with a version per GitHub release.
  • github-actions/<owner>/<repo> listed as an endpoint under Endpoints, if any workflow runs succeeded.

If both of those are present, your release and deployment metadata is flowing correctly. That’s expected at this stage even if you don’t see any CVEs yet — see Attach or Discover SBOMs and Sync Vulnerability Data for the next layer of data.

item.title: Architecture Guide
item.description:
Audience: Platform engineers, infrastructure teams, and security reviewers deploying or evaluating DeployHub on-premises.

For the data model itself (releases, SBOMs, CVEs, orgs, sync records), see the rest of Core Concepts. This page covers non-functional requirements, technology choices, and deployment topology.

Non-Functional Requirements

Performance and Scalability

The system is designed to handle large-scale vulnerability management workloads with optimal end-user experience. All API endpoints maintain an end-user response time of less than 3 seconds under normal load conditions, including:

item.title: Attach or Discover SBOMs
item.description:
GitHub onboarding imports release and deployment metadata — it does not attach an SBOM, so there is nothing yet for CVE matching to run against. No changes to your CI/CD pipeline, no CLI, and no direct API calls from your pipeline are required to fix this — SBOM data reaches DeployHub automatically via a scanner component:

  • relscanner-job — a CronJob that discovers releases from GitHub/GitLab and auto-attaches an SBOM via OCI attestations, Cosign, GitHub Release assets, or Syft/cdxgen generation.

Ask your platform team whether this is deployed alongside your backend — that’s the only setup step. Once it is, give it up to one scan cycle (15 minutes by default) after a repo is connected.

item.title: Track Deployments
item.description:
DeployHub tracks deployments — where a release is actually running right now — separately from release metadata. There are a few ways this data arrives:

  • GitHub Actions workflow runs: onboarding a repository (see Select Repositories to Watch) automatically creates a github-actions/<owner>/<repo> endpoint and sync record for each successful workflow run.
  • relscanner-job: the same scanner component that discovers SBOMs also detects deployments from GitHub/GitLab CI, container images, and published releases. See Deployment Tracking Using GitHub and GitLab.
  • GKE deployments: if you run on Google Kubernetes Engine, a separate component tails Cloud Audit Logs to detect new pod deployments automatically. See Deployment Tracking for GKE.

You generally don’t need to configure this yourself beyond connecting GitHub/GitLab or deploying the relevant scanner component — ask your platform team which of these is running in your environment.

item.title: Sync Vulnerability Data
item.description:
CVE data is refreshed from osv.dev every 15 minutes by default, by a component called the OSV Loader (osvdev-job). This runs independently of your own repositories — it’s a continuous background sync, not something you trigger per-release.

Supported ecosystems

Supported ecosystems for CVE matching: npm, PyPI, Maven, Go, NuGet, RubyGems, cargo (crates.io), Composer, apk (Alpine/Wolfi), deb (Debian/Ubuntu). All ecosystems published in the OSV ecosystem index are processed automatically — no configuration is required to add a new one.

item.title: First Success Checklist
item.description:
Use this checklist to confirm your initial setup is complete and data is flowing correctly. Each item links to the relevant setup step if you need to revisit it.

  • Account created and you can log in — see Log In for the First Time
  • GitHub connected — see Connect GitHub
  • Repositories selected and onboarded — see Select Repositories to Watch
  • Releases and endpoints appear on your dashboard — see Verify Your Data
  • SBOM data attached to at least one release — see Attach or Discover SBOMs
  • Deployment tracking configured, if applicable — see Track Deployments
  • Vulnerability sync confirmed — CVE counts appear against your components — see Sync Vulnerability Data
  • Dashboard reads correctly — you understand the executive summary cards and severity breakdown — see Read Your Dashboard
  • Team invited — colleagues can log in with appropriate roles — see Invite Your Team

If you’re seeing zero CVEs after completing the above, see the troubleshooting note in Sync Vulnerability Data — SBOM data typically needs one scan cycle (about 15 minutes) after a repo is connected before CVE matching appears.

item.title: Get Support
item.description:
• GitHub Issues — ortelius/ortelius is used for issues across the whole project, including the backend, frontend, and scanner components.
  • Discord — join the community at discord.gg/ortelius for questions and discussion.
  • Documentation feedback — if a page in these docs is wrong or missing something, open an issue on DeployHubProject/DeployHub-Docs.
  • Security disclosures — email security@www.deployhub.com.

If you’re not sure whether something is a bug, a configuration issue, or expected behavior, check Troubleshooting and the FAQ first.

Want to contribute back rather than just ask for help? See the Contributor Guide.
